action: Generate the three missing figure PDFs (fig4_patching_comparison.pdf, fig5_pathological_histogram.pdf, fig6_architecture_comparison.pdf) from source data files. These correspond to three of five main contributions and block compilation.

Create a single authoritative data manifest mapping every number in the paper to exactly one data file and field. Resolve the three known discrepancies: (a) first-letter weighted F1 at L24 (paper says 0.97, full-mode data says 1.0), (b) benign/pathological instance count (paper 1,471 vs. consolidation 1,464), (c) first-letter L24_16k absorption rate (paper 27.1% per-unique-word vs. consolidation 34.5% per-instance).

Reframe the benign/pathological diagnostic: change 'absorption is always pathological' to 'absorption is pathological across all 1,471 tested instances from city-continent at L24.' Add a paragraph discussing the methodological limitation: the diagnostic ablates decoder geometry, not computational dependence. Acknowledge that this is a measure of absorption magnitude, not a proof of computational harm.

Clarify the Kruskal-Wallis test scope: explicitly state that p=7.4e-66 covers only 3 RAVEL hierarchies (excluding first-letter). Separate the descriptive claim ('rates range from 11.6% to 45.1%') from the inferential claim ('only city-language differs significantly from first-letter after Bonferroni correction at 16k'). Consider running a 4-hierarchy Kruskal-Wallis test if feasible.

Add a sentence to Section 3.3 documenting the aggregation method: 'Absorption rates are computed per unique entity (word or city), averaging across prompt contexts, to prevent entities with more prompt templates from dominating the rate.' Also report the alternative per-instance rate for first-letter (0.3448) in a footnote.

Report the probe-only baseline (control 3 from Section 3.3) numerically for each hierarchy. What is the false negative rate on raw activations alone? This bounds the probe-induced inflation of absorption rates. If this data was collected but not reported, include it. If not collected, acknowledge its absence and estimate the probe-quality-adjusted absorption rates.

Run the benign/pathological diagnostic on first-letter (the only hierarchy with F1=1.0 probes) to test whether the 100% pathological finding generalizes. If the finding is 100% pathological for first-letter too, the universality claim is strengthened. If benign instances exist in first-letter (concentrated absorption regime), this is a new and interesting finding about the mechanistic divide.

For the cross-domain patching failure (Section 5.2), test whether identified child features have above-chance cosine similarity with the parent probe direction. Also test whether zeroing the top-5 or top-10 attributed features improves recovery. This distinguishes 'distributed absorption' from 'broken child identification.'
Convert Table 3 from inline markdown to the same PDF format as other tables. Fix format inconsistency that will break LaTeX compilation.

Temper the architecture comparison conclusion from 'hierarchy dominates architecture' to 'we could not detect an architecture effect with the available data (p=0.50, n=12).' Acknowledge that the test is underpowered and the width mismatch confounds the comparison.

Clarify first-letter probe F1 ambiguity: if weighted F1 at L24 is 1.0 (as full-mode data shows), report 1.0 instead of 0.97. If 0.97 is correct for a different reason (e.g., including letter x with zero support), add a footnote explaining.

Add a note on multiple testing correction across the 4 Kruskal-Wallis tests conducted (hierarchy at L12, hierarchy at L24, architecture at L12, architecture at L24). State that Bonferroni correction does not affect any conclusion.
Document the token position asymmetry (first-letter: position -6, RAVEL: position -2) and explain why different positions are used. Acknowledge this as a confound if no control is available.
Trim Section 2.6: remove SynthSAEBench and CE-Bench citations (not used in the paper) or explain their relevance to the cross-domain gap.

Standardize the mean logit change number: paper says 3.98, consolidation says 3.992. Use 3.99 for consistency with source data (or 4.0 to two significant figures). Verify the '1000x' claim: 3.99/0.004 = 997.5, which rounds to 1,000x.
